items.question: Define population covariance Cov(X, Y) and give an equivalent formula using expectations.
items.answer: Cov(X, Y) = E[(X - E[X])(Y - E[Y])] = E[XY] - E[X]E[Y], measuring average joint deviation from the means in original units.

items.question: How is the Pearson correlation coefficient related to covariance?
items.answer: ρ(X, Y) = Cov(X, Y) / (σ_X σ_Y) when both standard deviations are positive. It standardizes covariance to [-1, 1] and is invariant to positive scaling of either variable.

items.question: Why can Cov(X, Y) = 0 while X and Y are dependent?
items.answer: Covariance captures only linear co-movement around means. Non-linear dependence (e.g., Y = X² with symmetric X) can cancel positive and negative products, yielding zero covariance despite deterministic linkage.

items.question: How do you obtain a sample covariance matrix from an n × p data matrix X?
items.answer: Center columns: X̃ = X - row mean vector. Sample covariance C = (1/(n - 1)) X̃ᵀ X̃. Entry C_ij is sample Cov(feature i, feature j); C_ii is sample variance of feature i.

items.question: When would you run PCA on the correlation matrix instead of the covariance matrix?
items.answer: When features are on incomparable scales and you want principal directions based on linear association structure rather than raw variance magnitude—typically after standardizing each feature to unit variance so Σ becomes R.
